ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='Good, so I can see:
1. Source keys use `CUST-XXXX` format for accounts - consistent across project__c.account__c and account.id
2. Opportunity keys use `OPP-XXXX` format - consistent with project__c.opportunity__c
3. Dates come in multiple formats: `YYYYMMDD`, `YYYY-MM-DD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, `DD.MM.YYYY`, and sentinel values like `N/A` or `0000-00-00`

The source project__c table has these columns: id, name, project_status__c, go_live_date__c, account__c, opportunity__c
No CreatedDate or LastModifiedDate in the source - I'll need to set defaults (CURRENT_DATE or NULL) and IsDeleted = 0.

For the status mapping:
- "Aktiv" → "Active"
- "Planung" → "In Planning" 
- "In Planung" → "In Planning"
- "Abgeschlossen" → "Completed"
- "completed"/"Completed" → "Completed"
- "Storniert" → "Cancelled"
- "cancelled"/"Cancelled" → "Cancelled"
- "Active"/"active" → "Active"
- "In Planning"/"In planning" → "In Planning"
- "On Hold"/"on hold" → "On Hold"
- "Pausiert" → "On Hold"

For dates, I need to handle multiple formats with a CASE-based approach using regex to detect format and TO_DATE accordingly.

Since there's no CreatedDate/LastModifiedDate in source, I'll use NULL (or CURRENT_TIMESTAMP) - the spec says text type so I can put a reasonable default. For IsDeleted, integer not null means 0 is appropriate.

Let me now construct the dbt model SQL for Project__c.', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='final_result', args='{"target_table": "Project__c", "dbt_sql": "{{ config(materialized='table') }}\n\nSELECT\n    CAST(id AS TEXT) AS \"Id\",\n    INITCAP(TRIM(name)) AS \"Name\",\n    CASE LOWER(TRIM(project_status__c))\n        WHEN 'aktiv' THEN 'Active'\n        WHEN 'active' THEN 'Active'\n        WHEN 'planung' THEN 'In Planning'\n        WHEN 'in planung' THEN 'In Planning'\n        WHEN 'in planning' THEN 'In Planning'\n        WHEN 'abgeschlossen' THEN 'Completed'\n        WHEN 'completed' THEN 'Completed'\n        WHEN 'storniert' THEN 'Cancelled'\n        WHEN 'cancelled' THEN 'Cancelled'\n        WHEN 'pausiert' THEN 'On Hold'\n        WHEN 'on hold' THEN 'On Hold'\n        ELSE NULL\n    END AS \"Project_Status__c\",\n    CASE\n        WHEN go_live_date__c IS NULL OR TRIM(go_live_date__c) = '' THEN NULL\n        WHEN go_live_date__c ~ '^\\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(go_live_date__c, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT\n        WHEN go_live_date__c ~ '^\\d{4}-\\d{2}-\\d{2}$' THEN CAST(TO_DATE(go_live_date__c, 'YYYY-MM-DD') AS TEXT)\n        WHEN go_live_date__c ~ '^\\d{1,2}/\\d{1,2}/\\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(go_live_date__c, 'MM/DD/YYYY')::TEXT\n        WHEN go_live_date__c ~ '^\\d{1,2}\\.\\d{1,2}\\.\\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(go_live_date__c, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT\n        ELSE NULL\n    END AS \"Go_Live_Date__c\",\n    CAST(account__c AS TEXT) AS \"Account__c\",\n    CAST(opportunity__c AS TEXT) AS \"Opportunity__c\",\n    CAST(id AS TEXT) AS \"Legacy_Project_ID__c\",\n    NULL AS \"CreatedDate\",\n    NULL AS \"LastModifiedDate\",\n    0 AS \"IsDeleted\"\nFROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'project__c') }}", "column_notes": [{"explanation": "Direct cast from source id; unique key for Project__c", "source_columns": ["id"], "target_column": "Id"}, {"explanation": "Trimmed and INITCAP'd for consistent formatting", "source_columns": ["name"], "target_column": "Name"}, {"explanation": "Mapped German/English variants to enum domain: Aktiv\u2192Active, Planung/In Planung\u2192In Planning, Abgeschlossen\u2192Completed, Storniert\u2192Cancelled, Pausiert\u2192On Hold; NULL for unmapped", "source_columns": ["project_status__c"], "target_column": "Project_Status__c"}, {"explanation": "Parsed multiple formats (YYYYMMDD, YYYY-MM-DD, MM/DD/YYYY, DD.MM.YYYY) via TO_DATE; NULL for N/A or unparseable", "source_columns": ["go_live_date__c"], "target_column": "Go_Live_Date__c"}, {"explanation": "Source account key uses same CUST-XXXX format as Account.Id; kept as-is (no join needed since Account model also maps CUST-XXXX \u2192 Id)", "source_columns": ["account__c"], "target_column": "Account__c"}, {"explanation": "Source opportunity key in OPP-XXXX format matches Opportunity.Id directly", "source_columns": ["opportunity__c"], "target_column": "Opportunity__c"}, {"explanation": "Populated from source natural id for row-level verification", "source_columns": ["id"], "target_column": "Legacy_Project_ID__c"}, {"explanation": "Source has no created date; set to NULL", "source_columns": [], "target_column": "CreatedDate"}, {"explanation": "Source has no modified date; set to NULL", "source_columns": [], "target_column": "LastModifiedDate"}, {"explanation": "Source has no soft-delete flag; defaulting to 0", "source_columns": [], "target_column": "IsDeleted"}], "reasoning": "The source project__c table directly contains all needed fields with keys already in compatible format (CUST-XXXX for accounts, OPP-XXXX for opportunities). The main transformations are: (1) mapping German/English status values to the enum domain, (2) parsing dates from 4 different formats using regex guards + TO_DATE, and (3) setting NULL/sentinel defaults for metadata columns not present in source.", "generation_failed": false}', tool_call_id='call_jee2s3f6')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=4842, output_tokens=1457), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 10, 27, 26, 501071, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 10, 22, 47, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-2578ae94-8edf-4dfa-bf7c-614042c2dd98', finish_reason='tool_call')

messages.raw:
ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 10, 27, 26, 507542, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Installed_Asset__c`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL
- `billingcountry` text NULL
- `legacy_customer_id__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact  (244 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `firstname` text NULL
- `lastname` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `phone` text NULL
- `title` text NULL
- `role__c` text NULL

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).

- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 10, 27, 26, 507542, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Installed_Asset__c`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:

- `Account__c` text

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).

- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='Interesting! Looking at the date values, I see "13.06.2030" and "13.08.2029" — if these were MM/DD format, month 13 would be invalid. So clearly the dot-separated dates are DD.MM.YYYY (European format), which makes sense. And the slash-separated dates like "1/13/2028", "1/24/2031" etc. — if interpreted as MM/DD/YYYY, month 1 is January with days 13, 24, etc., which is valid US format.

But wait, there's an ambiguity: "2/2/2028" could be either. Given that the dot-separated ones are clearly European (DD.MM.YYYY), and slash-separated ones like "1/13/2028" can only be MM/DD/YYYY (since day 13 can't be a month), I'll treat:
- Slash-separated as MM/DD/YYYY (US format)
- Dot-separated as DD.MM.YYYY (European format)

Now I also see that "13.06.2030" and similar values exist, which confirms the dot separator is DD.MM.YYYY (day 13 is possible).

There's also an interesting case: looking at the distinct_dates count of 397 out of 480 total rows — meaning there are 83 rows with duplicate dates or invalid dates.

Let me check for non-parseable values more thoroughly.', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='query_postgres', args='{"sql": "SELECT DISTINCT warranty_end_date__c FROM \"fixture_messy_data_v2_src\".\"installed_asset__c\" WHERE warranty_end_date__c NOT IN ('0000-00-00') AND warranty_end_date__c ~ '^\\d{4}-\\d{2}-\\d{2}$' OR warranty_end_date__c ~ '^\\d{1,2}/\\d{1,2}/\\d{4}$' OR warranty_end_date__c ~ '^\\d{1,2}\\.\\d{1,2}\\.\\d{4}$' OR warranty_end_date__c ~ '^\\d{8}$'"}', tool_call_id='call_rfgsxs9u')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=10513, output_tokens=492), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 10, 27, 41, 752010, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 10, 24, 20, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-b07b7bd2-46e1-406d-9bb4-b427bc566bd1', finish_reason='tool_call')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 10, 27, 26, 507542, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Installed_Asset__c`.

- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 10, 27, 26, 507542, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Installed_Asset__c`.

# Source database

- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 10, 27, 26, 507542, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Installed_Asset__c`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.

# Target schema


## Account

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 10, 28, 12, 352633, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Account` failed.

Previous SQL:
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT 
    -- Salesforce-style 15-char Account Id derived from legacy CUST-XXXX key
    'A00' || LPAD(SUBSTRING(id FROM '\d+')::INTEGER, 12, '0') AS "Id",
    
    -- Name: NOT NULL fallback for the ~6 NULL rows in source
    COALESCE(TRIM(name), 'Unknown Account') AS "Name",
    
    -- ERP_Number__c: pass-through (format: ERP-XXXXX)
    erp_number__c AS "ERP_Number__c",
    
    -- Customer_Tier__c: normalise German/English variants + casing to enum domain
    CASE LOWER(TRIM(customer_tier__c))
        WHEN 'platin'   THEN 'Platinum'
        WHEN 'platinum' THEN 'Platinum'
        WHEN 'gold'     THEN 'Gold'
        WHEN 'bronze'   THEN 'Bronze'
        WHEN 'silber'   THEN 'Silver'
        WHEN 'silver'   THEN 'Silver'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Customer_Tier__c",
    
    -- Region__c: pass-through (already clean: Benelux, DACH, Nordics, Southern Europe, UK)
    region__c AS "Region__c",
    
    -- Industry: normalise German labels to English equivalents
    CASE LOWER(TRIM(industry))
        WHEN 'finanzen'            THEN 'Finance'
        WHEN 'gesundheitswesen'    THEN 'Healthcare'
        WHEN 'industrie'           THEN 'Manufacturing'
        WHEN 'technologie'         THEN 'Technology'
        WHEN 'finance'             THEN 'Finance'
        WHEN 'healthcare'          THEN 'Healthcare'
        WHEN 'it'                  THEN 'IT'
        WHEN 'manufacturing'       THEN 'Manufacturing'
        WHEN 'technology'          THEN 'Technology'
        ELSE industry
    END AS "Industry",
    
    -- Website: pass-through
    website AS "Website",
    
    -- BillingCity: pass-through (may contain umlauts - keep for fidelity)
    billingcity AS "BillingCity",
    
    -- BillingCountry: pass-through (German country names)
    billingcountry AS "BillingCountry",
    
    -- Legacy key for row-level verification
    id AS "Legacy_Customer_ID__c",
    
    -- CreatedDate / LastModifiedDate: not present in source → NULL
    CAST(NULL AS TEXT) AS "CreatedDate",
    CAST(NULL AS TEXT) AS "LastModifiedDate",
    
    -- IsDeleted: default to 0 (not deleted)
    0 AS "IsDeleted"

FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'account') }}
```

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content="The user wants me to fix the Account model. The error was:

- One model file per target table under `models/`.

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.

- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`

Let me extract the numeric part from "CUST-1001" → "1001", then pad it to 12 digits: `'A00' || LPAD('1001', 12, '0')` = `'A00' || '000000001001'` = `'A0000000001001'` (that's only 15 chars: A00 + 12 digits = 15).

Let me verify: `LPAD('1001', 12, '0')` = `'000000001001'` (12 chars), then `'A00' || '000000001001'` = `'A0000000001001'` (15 chars). That works.

The fix is to cast the SUBSTRING result back to TEXT before passing to LPAD:
`'A00' || LPAD(SUBSTRING(id FROM '\d+')::TEXT, 12, '0')`

Wait, actually `SUBSTRING(id FROM '\d+')` returns text already. Then `::INTEGER` casts it to integer. Then LPAD expects text. So I need either:

- Or extract as text and let LPAD handle it

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 10, 29, 13, 231785, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Opportunity` failed.

Previous SQL:
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    o.id AS "Id",
    o.name AS "Name",
    -- Stage mapping: normalize all source variants to the 10 allowed enum values
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) IN ('closed won', 'won', 'gewonnen', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) IN ('closed lost', 'lost', 'verloren', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) IN ('prospecting', 'prospect', 'prospec...ting', 'prospektion') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) IN ('qualification', 'quali', 'qualifikation') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) = 'in kontakt' THEN 'Qualification'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    -- CloseDate: handle YYYYMMDD, MM/DD/YYYY, DD.MM.YYYY, and ISO formats
    CASE
        WHEN o.closedate IS NULL THEN NULL
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN
            TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN
            TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN
            TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN
            CAST(o.closedate AS DATE)::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    -- Amount: strip currency text/prefixes, handle European format (X.XXX,XX)
    CASE
        WHEN o.amount IS NULL OR TRIM(o.amount) = '' THEN NULL
        ELSE
            CAST(
                REGEXP_REPLACE(
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        REGEXP_REPLACE(
                            LOWER(TRIM(o.amount)),
                            '(usd|eur|gbp|chf|€|£|$|%|\d*\s)', '', 'gi'
                        ),
                        -- Detect European format: dot before comma (thousands separator)
                        -- e.g. "60.702,05" → remove dots → "60702,05" → swap comma to dot → "60702.05"
                        '(\d+)\.(\d{3}),(\d+)', '\1\2.\3', 'g'
                    ),
                    -- Remove any remaining non-numeric chars except minus and dot
                    '[^\d.-]', '', 'g'
                )
            AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    -- CurrencyIsoCode: normalize symbols/names to ISO codes
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode)) IN ('usd', 'us dollar', 'us dollars', '$') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode)) IN ('eur', 'euro', '€') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode)) IN ('gbp', 'british pound', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode)) IN ('chf', 'swiss franc') THEN 'CHF'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode)) IN ('dollar', 'dollars') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN o.currencyisocode IS NULL OR TRIM(o.currencyisocode) = '' THEN NULL
        ELSE UPPER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode))
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    -- AccountId: direct join to Account.Id (both use CUST-XXXX format)
    o.accountid AS "AccountId",
    -- Legacy key for row-level verification
    o.id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"

FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }} o
```

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model

- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).

```sql

        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) IN ('closed lost', 'lost', 'verloren', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) IN ('prospecting', 'prospect', 'prospec...ting', 'prospektion') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) IN ('qualification', 'quali', 'qualifikation') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) = 'in kontakt' THEN 'Qualification'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    -- CloseDate: handle YYYYMMDD, MM/DD/YYYY, DD.MM.YYYY, and ISO formats
    CASE
        WHEN o.closedate IS NULL THEN NULL
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN
            TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN
            TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN
            TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN
            CAST(o.closedate AS DATE)::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    -- Amount: strip currency text/prefixes, handle European format (X.XXX,XX)
    CASE
        WHEN o.amount IS NULL OR TRIM(o.amount) = '' THEN NULL
        ELSE
            CAST(
                REGEXP_REPLACE(
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(

                        '(\d+)\.(\d{3}),(\d+)', '\1\2.\3', 'g'
                    ),
                    -- Remove any remaining non-numeric chars except minus and dot
                    '[^\d.-]', '', 'g'
                )
            AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    -- CurrencyIsoCode: normalize symbols/names to ISO codes
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode)) IN ('usd', 'us dollar', 'us dollars', '$') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode)) IN ('eur', 'euro', '€') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode)) IN ('gbp', 'british pound', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode)) IN ('chf', 'swiss franc') THEN 'CHF'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode)) IN ('dollar', 'dollars') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN o.currencyisocode IS NULL OR TRIM(o.currencyisocode) = '' THEN NULL
        ELSE UPPER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode))
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    -- AccountId: direct join to Account.Id (both use CUST-XXXX format)
    o.accountid AS "AccountId",
    -- Legacy key for row-level verification
    o.id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"

FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }} o
```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 10, 29, 13, 231785, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Opportunity` failed.

Previous SQL:
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    o.id AS "Id",
    o.name AS "Name",
    -- Stage mapping: normalize all source variants to the 10 allowed enum values
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) IN ('closed won', 'won', 'gewonnen', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) IN ('closed lost', 'lost', 'verloren', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) IN ('prospecting', 'prospect', 'prospec...ting', 'prospektion') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) IN ('qualification', 'quali', 'qualifikation') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) = 'in kontakt' THEN 'Qualification'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    -- CloseDate: handle YYYYMMDD, MM/DD/YYYY, DD.MM.YYYY, and ISO formats
    CASE
        WHEN o.closedate IS NULL THEN NULL
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN
            TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN
            TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN
            TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN
            CAST(o.closedate AS DATE)::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    -- Amount: strip currency text/prefixes, handle European format (X.XXX,XX)
    CASE
```

- Account `id` is in format "CUST-XXXX" - exactly matching the Opportunity's `accountid` format!

The issue with the previous model was just the Amount casting error. The fix is to use `NULLIF(cleaned_amount, '')` before casting to DOUBLE PRECISION.

Let me also verify that my StageName mapping handles all edge cases. Looking at the data:
- "closed won", "LOST", "In Prüfung", "Gewonnen", "Abgeschlossen (Gewonnen)", "Qualifikation", "Prospect", "PROSPECTING", "In Kontakt", "Abgeschlossen (Verloren)", "Quali"

My previous mapping should handle all of these. Let me double-check:
- 'closed won', 'won', 'gewonnen', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)' → 'Closed Won' ✓
- 'closed lost', 'lost', 'verloren', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)' → 'Closed Lost' ✓  
- 'prospecting', 'prospect', 'prospektion' → 'Prospecting' ✓
- 'qualification', 'quali' → 'Qualification' ✓
- 'in prüfung' → 'Needs Analysis' ✓
- 'in kontakt' → 'Qualification' ✓

For CurrencyIsoCode, the source values include: "Euro", "Dollar", "chf", "USD", "£", "EUR", "GBP", "€", "usd"
My mapping handles all of these.

Now let me write the corrected model with the Amount fix using NULLIF:

```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    o.id AS "Id",
    o.name AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) IN ('closed won', 'won', 'gewonnen', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) IN ('closed lost', 'lost', 'verloren', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) IN ('prospecting', 'prospect', 'prospektion') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) IN ('qualification', 'quali') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.stagename)) = 'in kontakt' THEN 'Qualification'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN o.closedate IS NULL THEN NULL
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN
            TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN
            TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN
            TO_DATE(o.closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN o.closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN
            CAST(o.closedate AS DATE)::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN TRIM(o.amount) IS NULL OR TRIM(o.amount) = '' THEN NULL
        ELSE CAST(
            NULLIF(REGEXP_REPLACE(
                REGEXP_REPLACE(
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        LOWER(TRIM(o.amount)),
                         '(usd|eur|gbp|chf|€|£|$|%|\d*\s)', '', 'gi'
                     ),
                     '(\d+)\.(\d{3}),(\d+)', '\1\2.\3', 'g'
                 ),
                 '[^\d.-]', '', 'g'
             ), '') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode)) IN ('usd', 'us dollar', 'us dollars', '$') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode)) IN ('eur', 'euro', 'euros', '€') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode)) IN ('gbp', 'british pound', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode)) IN ('chf', 'swiss franc') THEN 'CHF'
        WHEN o.currencyisocode IS NULL OR TRIM(o.currencyisocode) = '' THEN NULL
        ELSE UPPER(TRIM(o.currencyisocode))
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    o.accountid AS "AccountId",
    o.id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"

FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }} o
```
